title: F# snippets web site link is incorrect in ReadMe
body: it's pointing to http://wwww.fssnip.net/ instead of http://www.fssnip.net/

title: fsharp code snippet in .md file
body:
I am not sure why [this change](https://github.com/nrolland/FSharp.Formatting/commit/15021128cbec81801e8514d47fe0226729f43cde) breaks the build script. it only adds a fsharp snippet in a md file.

Based on the documentation [here](http://tpetricek.github.com/FSharp.Formatting/demo.html), it should be ok..

title: use Fake ?
body:
Would this project no benefit from using Fake ?

what do you think ?

title: Line numbers in generated F# snippets in html
body:
It would be nice if when selecting the content of the F# snippets embedded in the generated html the line numbers weren't copied also. Most embedded code on blogs do that either by using embedded gists from http://gist.github.com or some variation of this javascript lib http://alexgorbatchev.com/SyntaxHighlighter/
Both fssnip and all the html generated by literate.fsx have this problem. In fssnip it's not a problem because you have the copy source button

title: Support of markdown extensions
body:
Tomas,

A)
I want to suggest to support tables and extend pure markdown syntax. The format of tables can be taken from [github](https://github.com/adam-p/markdown-here/wiki/Markdown-Cheatsheet#wiki-tables) and/or [pandoc](johnmacfarlane.net/pandoc/README.html#simple-tables).

I've added support of simple tables in my `pandoc-tables` branch of FSharp.Formatting 

```
------ --------------- ----------
**Id** **Description** **Link**
1      _text_          [url](url)
2      *text*          [url](url)
```

I also would like to extend it to support multiline cells.

B) 
In one of my current projects I'm using pandoc to generate both pdf and html documents from the same source. Documents contain math expressions so i use pandoc [math extension](http://johnmacfarlane.net/pandoc/README.html#math). The syntax is similar to the one used on [stackexchange](http://math.stackexchange.com/editing-help#latex). I want to switch from pandoc to FSharp.Markdown in this project but need support of math expressions.

What do you think about this features (tables and mathematics)? If you agree that they are useful then I will work on them (we also should discuss syntax in this case).

title: Generate PDF output
body:
Hi Tomas,

I often need PDF documents with F# syntax highlighting. I wonder whether we should generate PDF output directly from the tool.

We can generate a LaTeX document and use pdflatex to produce PDFs. The advantage is that we still can use templates as HTML generation. Its problem is the dependency on an external tool. However, unless we want to be something like [pandoc](http://johnmacfarlane.net/pandoc/), manipulating PDF on our own is not a good idea.

If we follow the LaTeX route, I would use [listings package](http://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/LaTeX/Source_Code_Listings#Using_the_listings_package) to color F# code. Tooltips make less sense in PDF format, but using [pdfcomment](http://tex.stackexchange.com/questions/34698/strange-appearance-of-tooltips-produced-by-pdfcomment) we are still able to see them in Acrobat Reader.

If you agree this is reasonable thing to do, I will try to come up with a pull request.

title: Support for double-backticks
body:
Hi Tomas,

I've spotted a small issue: when I use double-backticked functions, the tooltip is not displayed. Here is a small repro .fsx file:

let foo = ()

let ``f oo`` = ()

When the generated HTML is viewed, the first function will correctly display the tooltip, but the second won't.

If I get time I'll see if I can understand enough of the implementation to fix it.

Thanks
Akash

title: Future directions
body:
The tool is already useful as it is at current state. But I think there are still rooms for improvements. It would be beneficial if you (and others who are interested in) could share what you would like to improve further.

I think the tool is a great addition to F# ecosystem, and would like to contribute from time to time. Having a TODO list is great for that purpose.

From my experience working on the code base, there are two important things to do:
- Support Math extensions
- Create unit tests

Please share your thoughts.

title: Support Docco style literate code
body: It would be great if you could generate Docco style literate formatting of code.   I know you can already use the fsx generator on fs files but it would be great to have this option.  [marginalia](http://fogus.github.io/marginalia/) is a clojure example which also supports mathjax which would be another great feature.

title: Concept misunderstanding
body:
I am a newbie in `FSharp.Formatting` and try to understand how I can use it. I have some misunderstanding of overall concept and cannot find a way to build what I need.

As I understand, current goal of `Literate` is to transform `*.fsx` or `*.md` file to `*.html` according to template (one-to-one transformation). But, when I build a site, my primary goal is to get an `*.html` using some `*.fsx` and/or `.md` files (many-to-one transformation).

As a first step, I choose my page layout, after that I split my page into zones for different kinds of content and want to have an ability to `bind` zones to source `*.fsx`/`*.md` files. Something like this:

``` fsharp
let template = source + "page-template.html"
let zoneBindings=
  [ "main", "intro.md"
    "left", "fs-sample.fsx"
    "right", "cs-sample.md"]

Literate.BuildHTML
  (template, source + "\\output\\index.html", bindings = zoneBindings)
```

How can I build page with following layout using current version of `FSharp.Formatting.Literate`?
![image](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/1197905/778154/443710ca-e9a9-11e2-96b4-50274f17da18.png)

Please correct me if I am wrong or miss something.
Thanks.

title: Allow to embed the output of the F# code
body: In addition to calling the compiler to get the sytnax highlighting and tooltips, it would be great to have an option to also execute that code and include the output in the generated html. That would make writing tutorials nicer

title: Space after "*)"
body: If there is a space after `(** ... *)` in literate script, then things break...

title: Missing compiler directive from Markdown
body:
Hi,

I'm trying to port my FAKE docs to FSharp.Formatting. Unfortunately there is an issue with compiler directives in markdown files.

FSharp.Formatting converts #r to # (it removes the "r").

![image](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/57396/1236879/cb1ad024-29cb-11e3-8b0a-799dfb9ba86a.png)

See http://fsharp.github.io/FAKE/gettingstarted.html

title: Update CSS template for literate script
body: The current one does not handle line numbers correctly.

title: Render simple http.// links
body:
Hi,

in github's version of markdown you can simply write an URL (without any brackets) and it's rendered as a link. This would be nice for compatibility.

Cheers,
Steffen

title: Add more tests
body:
There are now separate [test projects](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/tree/master/tests), but most of them are still just `exe` files that you can run to test things by hand. We should turn those into unit tests...

The only one that has the right structure is [CodeFormat.Tests](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/tree/master/tests/FSharp.CodeFormat.Tests), so this can be used as an inspiration how the structure should look.

I'd welcome any contributions here :-) feel free to turn other projects in `tests` folder into proper Library projects that reference NUnit, include `FsUnit.fs` and contain some actual tests! 

For Markdown, it would be nice to compare against some existing tools (there are some sample outputs in `Benchmarks` folder) but we do not necessarily have to do exactly the same thing...

title: Problems when rendering globbing paths
body:
Hi,

for FAKE docs I want to render:

![image](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/57396/1332055/ed7bc574-356f-11e3-8ed7-71894764c28d.png)

but it creates:

![image](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/57396/1332062/116debe2-3570-11e3-9f69-a88193d464cb.png)

Cheers,
Steffen

title: Headings ending with # are not parsed correctly
body:
For example

```
## Hello F#
```

Becomes "Hello F"

title: "go to definition" for API docs
body:
Hi, I really like the new API documenting features. I'm already using them for Fake. 

One thing I'd like to see is a "go to definition" link for all generated function / module docs. 

This feature would allow me to browse from the docs directly to the github sources if I need further information. 

What do you think?

```

Output:

```
The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter is about 3.141592654...
```

Is it possible, or any future plans?

open FSharp.MetadataFormat

[<EntryPoint>]
let main argv = 
    generate ()
    0 // return an integer exit code

title: Add command line interface
body:
It would be nice to have a command line tool that can run the usual operations. Something along the lines of:

```
fsformatting metadataformat C:\my.dll C:\myother.dll C:\docs\reference
fsformatting literate C:\myscripts C:\docs
```

This would make it easier to integrate with FAKE (see https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/issues/75#issuecomment-29512370).

The exact format of command line arguments needs to be figured out (there is potentially a lot of them and some - like the parameters to be used in HTML replacements - are multi-line strings, so this might be tricky). Any suggestions how to do this are welcome.

title: Would be nice to be able to add a description to a namespace
body: Maybe adding something like `[namespace: description]` on the first top level type or module of the namespace could do the trick?

title: Change .nuspec to prevent NuGet from referencing all the .dlls
body:
Specifically, having NuGet auto-referencing the included FSharp.Core.dll causes problems (see https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/issues/78)
It's also annoying that `style.css` and `tips.js` are always copied to the project when updating NuGet.

See https://nuget.codeplex.com/wikipage?title=Allow%20Specifying%20Unreferenced%20Assemblies

title: Encapsulation of special chars
body:
```
* blub --> bla
```

this should be encaspulated as

```
* blub --> bla
```

see https://github.com/fsharp/FAKE/pull/259

/cc @cincuranet

title: Unicode symbols in Markdown

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Tasks that are up for grabs!

body: Unicode html tags like α `α` don't seem to work in .fsx or Markdown comments in code. Work-around with latex syntax is pretty simple though.

title: FSharp.Formatting seems to break with nested types.
body:
Something seems broken when nested types are involved.

I debugged the issue and found that the name `...Dictionary`2.KeyCollection...` is the problem. System.Type.GetType would expect `...Dictionary`2+KeyCollection...`, because KeyCollection is a nested type. The trouble is, that the code that actually renders the "." in the name is some parts deep inside the FSharp.PowerPack.Metadata.

I was trying to fix it, but I'm not perfectly sure, where to start. One option i considered was to extend the type of `NonLocalEntityRef of CcuThunk * string[]` to `NonLocalEntityRef of CcuThunk * (string * bool)[]`. But I'm not sure, if the code should even be called with Dictionary in the first place and maybe the there is something wrong.

Any suggestions are much appreciated.

title: Suggestion: move to fsprojects?
body:
Should this project move to github.com/fsprojects, to attract a wider range of contributors and appear in a more canonical location?

Cheers!
Don

title: Fix nuget package
body:
I just tried running FSharp.Formatting with the newest NuGet package and am getting a:

```
Could not load file or assembly 'FSharp.Compiler.Service, Version=0.0.11.0, bla bla...
```

error. And in fact, when I downloaded the Package, the FSharp.Compiler.Service 0.0.10.0 is in the package.

title: Transient problems with new FSharp.Formatting
body:
Sometimes I get errors like this:

> System.AggregateException: One or more errors occurred. ---> System.IO.IOException: The process cannot access the file 'C:\src\FSharp.Data\docs\output\reference\fsharp-data-runtime-structuralinference-seq.html' because it is being used by another process.

title: Handle duplicate types
body: Happens in FSharp.Data/FSharp.Data.Experimental

title: List descriptions for type providers and for their static parameters
body: Right know we're getting an empty page: See http://blog.codebeside.org/FSharp.Data/reference/fsharp-data-csvprovider.html

title: Improve F# signature formatting
body:
Take the following signature:

```
Signature: (untypedParse:ParseFileResults * filename * source * options * isResultObsolete * textSnapshotInfo:obj option) -> Async<CheckFileOption>
```

Once it gets to a certain width it becomes hard to read especially if it is forced to break lines in the middle.  Perhaps some formatting like this might be nice:

![screen shot 2014-01-21 at 09 57 44](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/588746/1962222/b80a00c0-8283-11e3-81a1-9cd6c660edf8.png)

colourising type names and the `->` could also improve legibility

title: private let bindings still appearing in docs in 2.3.5
body: See http://blog.codebeside.org/FSharp.Data/reference/fsharp-data-csv-csvfile.html and http://blog.codebeside.org/FSharp.Data/reference/fsharp-net-http.html

title: Constructor parameters are mistaken for record fields in classes in 2.3.5
body: See columns and parent in http://blog.codebeside.org/FSharp.Data/reference/fsharp-data-csv-csvrow.html

title: Would be nice to show Union Case arguments
body:
For example: http://blog.codebeside.org/FSharp.Data/reference/fsharp-data-json-jsonvalue.html

Would be nice to show `Float(float)` instead of just `Float`, making them display more like methods.
If it's the F# 3.1 named DUs, show the names, otherwise show just the type name.
Having to hover to get the types in method parameters is ok, as you already have the names that give you some information, but for DUs never showing anything makes it feel too basic

title: Functions with unit input render incorrectly in 2.3.5
body:
For example:

`let start () = printfn "start"`

is rendered as:

`start arg1`

title: Properties are displaying as methods in 2.3.5
body: Columns(arg1) in http://blog.codebeside.org/FSharp.Data/reference/fsharp-data-csv-csvrow.html

title: Optional tooltips and display function signature always
body:
Is there a way of disabling the tool tips and just render the function signature in plain HTML under the function name ?

I find annoying having to move the mouse to see the function signature.

If not I can look into doing this.

title: Strange member showing up on enums
body:
this:

```
/// Specifies formatting behaviour of JSON values
type SaveOptions = 
  /// Format (indent) the JsonValue
  | None = 0
  /// Print the JsonValue in one line
  | DisableFormatting = 1
```

produces this:

![image](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/738761/2071549/c23b827e-8d2a-11e3-8574-658eeff977d1.png)

Where does that value__ come from?

title: Fix failing Github source link test (error with file locations)
body: Looks like the test `MetadataFormat generates Go to GitHub source links` is failing on the Travis build for the current master. I thought this was because of absolute path, but it still fails even after I fixed the path [here](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/commit/4154c9db6cbfa47d744ae2bdabcee771356ed5f8). I added some logging so you can see more [in the Travis log](https://travis-ci.org/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting). It is called with `sourceFolder` that looks valid on Mac. See the following line from the output: ``` Using root folder: /Users/travis/build/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/tests/FSharp.MetadataFormat.Tests/../../ ``` But then it generates the following link: ``` Go to GitHub source ``` This looks a bit odd - is the issue that the library that we want to document has been built on my Win machine, but the doc generation is running on a Mac machine? That sounds like perhaps a bit unusual scenario, but perhaps it should work - or we need to change the build scripts to build `FsLib` as part of the build (and then it will generate, hopefully, the right metadata on Mac too?) @dungpa - I think you did some work on this feature - could you look into this?

title: Type (string*string) list appearing as numbers
Travis build failing
body:
The travis build seems to be failing - [see the log](https://travis-ci.org/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting).

Unhandled Exception

```
System.Runtime.Remoting.RemotingException: Tcp transport error.

```

Running build failed.

```
Error:
System.Exception: NUnit test failed (156).
  at Fake.NUnitSequential.NUnit (Microsoft.FSharp.Core.FSharpFunc`2 setParams, IEnumerable`1 assemblies) [0x00000] in <filename unknown>:0

  at Fake.TargetHelper.runTarget@290 (System.String targetName) [0x00000] in <filename unknown>:0

This appears to have something to do with how FAKE runs unit tests? But the build works fine on my WIndows machine and according to https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/issues/128#issuecomment-34546907, it also works when run from the Mac command line. So perhaps this is something specific to Travis?

/cc @forki

title: Support evaluation in *.md files
body:
We recently added support for evaluation of F# snippets: http://tpetricek.github.io/FSharp.Formatting/evaluation.html

However, this is tested (implemented?) only for literate script files. For completeness, we should test/implement the same functionality for literate Markdown files with embedded F# snippets.

title: Update to FCS 0.0.33
body: FCS 0.0.26 moves to 1-based line counting. Please move to that when you get a chance.

title: Assume an implicit [omit] on members with CompilerMessageAttribute(IsHidden=true)

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Tasks that are up for grabs!

title: Private record fields appear in the generated docs
body:
Example:

```
type JsonDocument = 

  private { Json : JsonValue
            Path : string }
```

![image](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/738761/2369342/8bf15db2-a7db-11e3-837c-e10931c1507a.png)

title: (** ## not parsed correctly
body:
This works:

```
(**
## Getting extra information
```

But this doesn't:

```
(** ## Getting extra information
```

title: A named param is confused with a toplevel value
body: ![image](https://f.cloud.github.com/assets/738761/2454936/caa4b1a6-aef4-11e3-929c-53393933eba0.png)

title: Links to files in Literate programming doc are broken.
body:
They all return "404 Not Found".

http://tpetricek.github.io/FSharp.Formatting/literate.html

title: Invalid github links when there's no source location available
body: See http://fsharp.github.io/FSharp.Data/reference/fsharp-data-csvprovider.html

title: The end line of github links is not right
body: Example: in http://fsharp.github.io/FSharp.Data/reference/fsharp-data-jsonvalue.html, The link for RequestAsync is https://github.com/fsharp/FSharp.Data/tree/master//src/Json/JsonValue.fs#L332-332, but it should instead be https://github.com/fsharp/FSharp.Data/tree/master/src/Json/JsonValue.fs#L332-344 (note there was also an extra /)

title: Extensions don't show on the generated reference docs
body: Example: http://fsharp.github.io/FSharp.Data/reference/fsharp-data-xelementextensions.html

title: When a (** comment is not closed, we get a crash in a substring call
body: It would be nice to get a descriptive exception

title: Allow to pass a dll only for referencing
body: In order for the documentation generation for ApiaryProvider to work, I also have to pass FSharp.Data.dll in the dllFiles param of MetadataFormat.Generate, otherwise it will give assembly resolution errors, but I don't want to generate docs for FSharp.Data. There should be a separate parameter just for the dependenceis

title: Fonts get alll messed up if docs are accessed via https instead of http
body:
Compare http://fsharp.github.io/FSharp.Data/ vs https://fsharp.github.io/FSharp.Data/
Happens on both IE and Chrome

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/738761/2801083/8ad8eeec-cc7d-11e3-9d9b-750ff527ebcf.png)

title: Would be nice to be able to auto generate a toc inside a page
body: I suggest commands `(** include-toc:1 **)`, `(** include-toc:2 **)`, and `(** include-toc:3 **)` to generate toc's with heading 1's, heading 1's and 2's, and heading's 1's, 2',s, and 3's, respectively

title: A value with surround ticks and comma is not parsed correctly in special command
body:
For example, I have something like this in a F# script file (*.fsx).

```
let ``P(R,C)`` = 10
(*** include-value:``P(R,C)`` ***)
```

The ValueReference is `P(R` instead of `(R,C)`

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/116863/2952849/b96f9e60-da45-11e3-80c6-2189b7afa376.png)

title: There is some issue with the generated documentation
body:
Doesn't embed the tooltips correctly.

e.g. https://tpetricek.github.io/FSharp.Formatting/literate.html

Due to a type that contains chars '<' and '>' or something like that..

title: First line of snippet not rendered
body:
In https://github.com/fsharp/FAKE/pull/494 I got:

```
["Something.fs"]
|> Fsc (fun p ->
          { p with Output = "Something.dll"
                   FscTarget = Library
                   OtherParams =
                      [ "--nooptimizationdata"
                        "--checked+" ] })
```

but it renders as 

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/57396/3486121/6a934298-041f-11e4-9bde-05b7fd36e661.png)

I assume it tries to detect the script language and therefor omits ["Something.fs"].

title: API generation fails when attempting to parse <Module> type in C# assemblies
body:
For whatever reason, the library attempts to generate documentation for the `<Module>` type. This causes a failure, since the ouput file contains invalid characters. 

This is the type record that caused the failure:

``` fsharp
{Name = "<Module>";
 UrlName = "global-<module>";
 Comment = {Blurb = "";
            FullText = "";
            Sections = [];};
 UnionCases = [];
 RecordFields = [];
 StaticParameters = [];
 AllMembers = [];
 Constructors = [];
 InstanceMembers = [];
 StaticMembers = [];}
```

title: Github Flavored Markdown
body:
There are a few enhancements I'd like to see:

Page links in the form of  `[[page name]]` does currently not work

Images in the form of `[[/images/foo.png]]` does currently not work

This form of sourcecode does not seem to work either (?)

```
'''csharp   
public void Foo()  //not indented
{
}
'''
```

where `'` should be backtick, dont know how to escape here..

title: Auto-generate anchors for markdown headers
body: Github already does this, would be nice to have it here too.

title: Make F# Formatting compatible with CommonMark

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Tasks that are up for grabs!

body:
There is an attempt to standardized Markdown at [CommonMark](http://commonmark.org/) which I think it's a good idea. I tried `FSharp.Formatting` with the [sample](http://johnmacfarlane.net/babelmark2/?text=%23+Hello+there%0A%0AThis+is+a+paragraph.%0A%0A-+one%0A-+two%0A-+three%0A-+four%0A%0A1.+pirate%0A2.+ninja%0A3.+zombie) that Jeff Atwood gives, and the output html is below. ```

Hello there

This is a paragraph.

  • one
  • two
  • three

  • four

  • pirate
  • ninja
  • zombie
``` I just want to throw the idea around. :-)

title: How can we check that all links are valid
body:
We would like to do something like http://thechangelog.com/rawler-crawl-your-website-and-find-broken-links-with-rub/ 
Is this already possible? 

cc @agross

title: Need to catch all throws for good error reporting
body:
This is a real problem when attempting to automate looping through folders to generate docs on a whole system. 

In particular is is possible to throw inside compiler services, in which case no useful information is returned on which token, line, or even file caused the problem.

Need to catch all errors and minimally report the offending file, better yet, line number and token as well.

(I started looking at this, but unfortunately do not have time to continue for the foreseeable future, so I am recording it here.)

title: Reference documentation generation with CSharp Extension methods fails

title: Apostrophes in indented code blocks are double-encoded and then highlighted
body:
Here's a quick repro of the problem. Ran into this while trying to use FsReveal to create a presentation in which I show examples in both F#, C# and JS. I can work around it for many things, but chars in C# are hard to do without apostrophes :). ``` #I "../packages/FSharp.Formatting.2.4.30/lib/net40" #I "../packages/RazorEngine.3.3.0/lib/net40" #I "../packages/FSharp.Compiler.Service.0.0.62/lib/net40" #r "FSharp.Markdown.dll" #r "FSharp.Literate.dll" #r "FSharp.CodeFormat.dll" open FSharp.Literate Literate.ParseScriptString """ (** Look ma', I'm good. [lang=js] var but = 'I\'m not so good...'; *) """ |> Literate.WriteHtml (*

Look ma', I'm good.

var but = 'I\'m not so good...';
*) ```

title: FCS dependency is not version restricted and latest FCS breaks stuff
body:
Ref fsprojects/ProjectScaffold#104 

Not sure what is the best approach here, main problem is that the latest FCS seems to be using stuff that is not yet available as a general download (i.e. the downloads of fsharp.org) and as such breaks Fsharp.formatting cause no upper limit is put on the FCS dependency.

title: DUs with private implementation still show constructors and union cases on the docs
finding XML file
body:
In FSharp.Metadata line 876 you have the xmlfile already but search then for all .*.xml files:

```
let xmlFile = defaultArg xmlFile (Path.ChangeExtension(dllFile, ".xml"))
let xmlFileOpt =
Directory.EnumerateFiles(Path.GetDirectoryName(xmlFile),
                                         Path.GetFileNameWithoutExtension(xmlFile) + ".*")
|> Seq.tryFind (fun f -> Path.GetExtension(f).Equals(".xml", StringComparison.CurrentCultureIgnoreCase))
match xmlFileOpt with
| None -> raise <| FileNotFoundException(sprintf "Associated XML file '%s' was not found." xmlFile)
| Some xmlFile -> ...
```

Why is it done like this? In our case it crashes our documentation, as we have two projects resulting in xml names: 
- MySolutionName.ProjectName1.xml
- MySolutionName.ProjectName1.abc.xml

The naming has historical reasons. The code around line 876 finds both time the second xml file, leaving the first project undocumented. We have a nasty workaround, but we would like to have some fix in fsformating. Do you have any suggestions?

title: Line numbers in C#
body:
Currently it's possible to add/remove line numbers from the F# snippets only, it would be nice to have a support for other languages as well. 
`SourceFormat` (and `CSharpFormat` in particular) have an option to generate the line numbers, but it's always set to default. The only issue with it is that html structure is different from F# one: you can't easily copy the code without numbers.

title: Key already exists exception when parsing an assembly
body:
When there's a documented instance member and interface implementation with the same name - `Name` - in the snippet below, MetadataFormat.Generate fails with an exception, because there're two identical member names in a .xml file (`<member name="P:TestFormatting.Test.Name">`) and no references are generated. As a workaround one of the comments has to be removed...

```
type ITest = abstract member Name : string

type Test (name) =
    /// instance comment
    member x.Name = name

    interface ITest with
        /// interface comment
        member x.Name = name  
```

Exception:
`System.ArgumentException: An element with the same key already exists in the dictionary.
  at System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary`2[System.String,System.Xml.Linq.XElement].Add (System.String key, System.Xml.Linq.XElement value) [0x00000] in <filename unknown>:0

title: [Question] What needs to be done to improve support for reference docs from C# assemblies
body:
(This is a follow up of the discussion in #217)

The current state of docs generated from C# assemblies is rather lacking.

While Fsharp.Formatting no longer crashers, it does not generate a lot of useful docs either

Earlier discussions seems to have ended with FCS being responsible for most of what is missing.
Adding to that, FSharp.Formatting is currently pinned to an older release of FCS due to some issues I can't quite remember.

I would very much like to help push the cross language support of FSharp.Formatting forward, but I need some guidance and pointers as to where I should start. Particularly if where I should start is by doing something to FCS.

As an alternative to the FCS route, we could go the complete separate route and explore whether using Roslyn for C# assemblies is a better way than trying to bend FCS into C# land.

I like what FSharp.Formatting can do with F# code, and I've yet to find a tool that provides the same nice result and ease of use, so I'd very much like to see good C# support aswell.

title: Simplify literate documentation examples in FSharp.Formatting
body:
## Problem

When writing F# inline documentation examples there's a lot of protocol because you need to close the comment and use things like `(*** [some metadata] ***)` and reopen the comment again...
## Inspiration

In Elixir writing documentation examples is a breeze, because they use standard markdown code to include snippets, which can get tested when you build your documentation. 
This allows a very succinct type of code documentation, where the examples always work.
## An example

After looking at the current approach F# uses and comparing this with my Elixir background, I created a small mock-up that would show what my (personal) ideal approach for documenting would look like.

The F# code would look like this:

`````` F#
    (** Sums a list of integers.

    ## Remarks

    - Produces an `ArgumentException` for an empty list. 
    - It would be better if we'd use `List.fold` with a `0`, so it doesn't throw upon an empty list.

    ## Examples

    ```
    > let somelist = [1;2;3;4];;
    > listsum somelist;;
    val it : int = 10 
    > listsum [];;
    System.ArgumentException...
    > listsum [4;5];;
    val it : int = 9
    ```
    *)
    let listsum = List.reduce (+)
``````
## Documentation extraction

This would be fairly standard markdown, with the exception of maybe adding the function header & type by default; it generate something like this:

`-------------------------------><8--------------------------`
## listsum:: int list -> int

Sums a list of integers.
## Remarks
- Produces an `ArgumentException` for an empty list. 
- It would be better if we'd use `List.fold` with a `0`, so it doesn't throw upon an empty list.
# Examples

``` F#
> let somelist = [1;2;3;4];;
> listsum somelist;;
val it : int = 10
> listsum [];;
System.ArgumentException...
> listsum [4;5];;
val it : int = 9
```

`-------------------------------><8--------------------------`
## Verification of the examples during the build of the documentation

Here's how the testing of the examples would go:
In a codeblock:
- If the source line starts with `>`:
  - execute it and store the output lines
  - If a source line doesn't start with `>`:
    - If the source line ends with `...` compare everything in front of it with the current output line
    - If it doesn't, compare the source line with the current output line
  - go to the next output line & source line
## Mentioned disadvantage

When discussing this approach on twitter, the lack of not having intellisense in your examples was mentioned. As I found out during my time with Elixir, that shouldn't really be an issue, because you usually write your examples in the REPL anyway so you can verify them, so it's just a simple copy and paste.

title: Handling of non-ascii characters
body:
Using html entities within code blocks to represent non-ascii characters seems to not be working. (Assuming that the reason is that html entities within `
` blocks are not expanded, but rendered as literates.)

Is there really any need for html entities when outpout is utf-8?

title: Customize parsed markup output.
body:
Sometimes you want to be able to change the output of the html generation: https://github.com/matthid/FSharp.Formatting/tree/prismjs_support

But a lot of this is hard-coded: https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/blob/master/src/FSharp.Markdown/HtmlFormatting.fs

So I'm suggesting to replace this code with RazorEngine templates. This way it would be easier for users to replace the templates. Would you accept this change? 
If there is still interest in a RazorEngine alternative we would want to keep the generation code as I don't think we can do the same with other template engines.

I can already think of some problems:
- `*.cshtml` templates are written in C# so the code will not be "nice and clean" because of F# interop
- It complicates things.
- There will a lot of new small templates, which all needs to be compiled which will lead to longer run-times (cold start)

But there would be some benefits:
- Users can edit single templates without the need to fork, edit and build FSharp.Formatting.
- One can use their own strategy to colorize code-blocks.
- Other outputs than Latex and Html are possible (by just using your own templates)
- no direct mathjax dependency for formulas

What do you think? I'm thinking about this some time now, but I'm not sure if it's worth the effort.

title: Use FSharpVSPowerTools.Core to syntactically colorize F# code snippets
body:
After I changed the color scheme used for code snippets to match VS light theme (mostly), I'll get the very boring result:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/873919/5887663/77372fe4-a3ef-11e4-9969-838471505016.png)

It's identical to what we have in VS without VFPT installed. We publish  [FSharpVSPowerTools.Core NuGet package](https://www.nuget.org/packages/FSharpVSPowerTools.Core/) which includes IDE-agnostic functionality. In particular, https://github.com/fsprojects/VisualFSharpPowerTools/blob/master/src/FSharpVSPowerTools.Core/SourceCodeClassifier.fs#L265 could be used to syntactically classify F# code to the following categories:

``` fsharp
type Category =
    | ReferenceType
    | ValueType
    | PatternCase
    | Function
    | MutableVar
    | Quotation
    | Module
    | Unused
    | Printf
    | Escaped
    | Other
```

/cc @dungpa

title: Cleanup RazorEngine files.
body:
Some background: https://github.com/Antaris/RazorEngine/issues/244

Basically RazorEngine was leaking temp files sine 3.5.0. Now I have a fix/workaround in place but it only works when RazorEngine is not used in the default `AppDomain`. So we need to take additional actions here in F# Formatting (because the primary usage is via FSI, which sadly uses the default AppDomain..).

Because spinning up AppDomains in F# scripts will be very difficult (to get a dynamic assembly from one into another AppDomain), I can only see two options:
- Spin up the AppDomain internally in F# Formatting (if we are in the default AppDomain)
  But I guess this is only possible for high level APIs like `MetadataFormat.Generate` (I don't think we can support high order functions)
- Handle this in FAKE: Spin up a new AppDomain within FAKE and run the build-script from there
  Of course we need to also change https://github.com/fsprojects/ProjectScaffold/blob/master/build.template#L199 and spin up another FAKE process instead of calling `fsi.exe`.
  At this point everything should work fine.

IMHO we should opt for 2 (it should be easier to implement as well) and leave F# Formatting unchanged, however I would like to hear what you think.
/cc @tpetricek @forki

title: Fix FSharp.Formatting dependency hell.
body:
This is a continuation of https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/issues/277, https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/issues/261 and https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/pull/239 to finally get rid of those.

IMHO we should only go down the ILMerge road if we have literally no other option, using ILMerge on libraries will probably increase the pain for library users which happen to have the same dependencies (FCS comes into mind). I think the notes of http://fsharp.github.io/2015/04/18/fsharp-core-notes.html apply basically for all other dependencies as well (TL;DR: libraries should not bundle and should not ILMerge dependencies).

The good thing is there are at least two other solutions:
- Create some kind of wrapper `.exe` like `FAKE.exe` which wraps the execution of our `generate.fsx`. With this we can basically configure our assembly redirects (for that exe) or even consider ILMerge (if we still want to do that for some reason). We could even have our own DSL and ask @forki how he would do it if he would re-do FAKE :). For the start if would probably be good to support existing scripts if possible.
- Do what you already did: Fix the versions and additionally bundle `all` dependencies. This is probably the lower cost solution.

Note how both options contradict my initial statement (we started contradicting it since bundling RazorEngine). So additionally we should split out our use cases in two seperate NuGet packages 
- FSharp.Formatting <- used as it is currently as a build time helper (with one of the above options) with no additional dependency (everything is bundled to "just work" in build scripts; with one of the options from above)
- FSharp.Formatting.Library <- used in case someone wants to use FSharp.Formatting as library, for example the markdown parser (bundles no dependencies but has nuget dependencies). Users of the library need to handle assembly redirection, just like for any other library.

_Sidenote (Paket):
We actually "could" get rid of the "FSharp.Formatting" nuget package if Paket would give us a way to install "FSharp.Formatting.Library" and all dependencies (without updating anything) in a separate folder. Something like isolated and separate "Build" dependencies come into mind... This resolution would need to be completely independent from project dependencies. But this is probably far away... This feature would probably help FAKE (which kind of has the same problem -> FAKE could probably get away with not bundling everything) as well. Kind of like https://github.com/fsprojects/Paket/issues/515 but without even the need to specify the dependencies you want to internalize (We always want to internalize everything). With manually specifying "internalize" we have problems like changing paths when noticing we need to internalize another dependency. And IMHO we should even internalize "FSharp.Formatting" itself (so users can use different versions for referencing and building if they want to)_

The problem mentioned in https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/pull/239 with ILMerging RazorEngine still applies (but I'm pretty sure we could workaround that with the latest versions if ILMerging is _really_ what you want)

I think I can come up with a PR for option 2 really quick, if needed/wanted. Maybe @forki can tell us if this "build" dependency stuff (sidenote) is something Paket can and wants to provide, but figuring out the details (and the implementation) will probably take some time anyway. If we go the "Paket" way I'm not sure how to support plain NuGet, but we can deal with that later on. 

Tell me what you think and which option you would prefer. Other options to finally fix the dependency hell are appreciated as well :)

title: FSharp.Literate does not escape HTML entities in code blocks with unknown language
body: When using e.g. `FSharp.Literate.ProcessDirectory` to generate HTML from Markdown, **code blocks with unknown languages** (e.g. `batchfile`) receive no escaping of special HTML characters (&, <, >). This issues seems to only affect code blocks with languages that are unknown to `CSharpFormat.SyntaxHighlighter.FormatCode`. Code blocks with known languages and inline code blocks do receive correct escaping. Contents: - **Reproduction steps** - **Details / Root cause** - **Possible solution** ## Reproduction steps In practice, that means that: ``` [lang=batchfile] program --argument ``` results in the following HTML: ``` html program --argument ``` instead of: ``` html program --argument <string> ``` ## Details / Root cause When a code snippet is to be formatted, the following steps occur (only relevant parts are included): 1. [`Transformations.replaceSpecialCodes`](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/blob/master/src/FSharp.Literate/Transformations.fs#L350) calls [`SyntaxHighlighter.FormatCode`](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/blob/master/src/CSharpFormat/SyntaxHighlighter.cs#L47) to turn the raw code into formatted HTML. 2. [`SyntaxHighlighter.FormatCode`](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/blob/master/src/CSharpFormat/SyntaxHighlighter.cs#L95) looks up the correct source formatter `sf` for the specified language. 1. _If the language is known_: `sf.FormatCode(string)` is called. The [default implementation](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/blob/master/src/CSharpFormat/SourceFormat.cs#L134) calls the private function [`SourceFormat.FormatCode`](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/blob/master/src/CSharpFormat/SourceFormat.cs#L187), which, besides other things, replaces special HTML characters (&, <, >) with the appropriate HTML entities. 2. _If the language is not known_: The raw code is returned without changes. **No encoding of special HTML characters is applied.** 3. [`Transformations.replaceSpecialCodes`](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/blob/master/src/FSharp.Literate/Transformations.fs#L350) writes the result of `SyntaxHighlighter.FormatCode` into the output (that is, either the HTML output from the formatter, or the unescaped code). ## Possible solution The solution for this problem is rather simple: Either [`SyntaxHighlighter.FormatCode`](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/blob/master/src/CSharpFormat/SyntaxHighlighter.cs#L95) or [`Transformations.replaceSpecialCodes`](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/blob/master/src/FSharp.Literate/Transformations.fs#L350) calls [`SyntaxHighlighter.FormatCode`](https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting/blob/master/src/CSharpFormat/SyntaxHighlighter.cs#L47) should apply escaping of special HTML characters even if the language is not known. In my opinion, the change should be applied to `SyntaxHighlighter`, because the safe escaping of the output should not depend on whether the language is known or not. On the other hand, if `CSharpFormat.SyntaxHighlighter` is considered to be a public API, the change might break some clients outside of `FSharp.Formatting` that expect `SyntaxHighlighter.FormatCode` to return the unescaped input if the language is not known (e.g. because they already use a workaround for the issue described here). But it is also likely that other clients produce invalid HTML because they do not employ a workaround.

title: Exception of type 'Microsoft.FSharp.Compiler.Tast+InternalUndefinedItemRef' when generating reference docs for an assembly that uses the Json type provider.
body:
I'm using FSharp.Formatting as implemented in the ProjectScaffold to generate reference documentation. When attempting to generate reference documentation, I'm getting an exception when MetadataFormat.Generate is called. By process of elimination I found that removing all JsonProviders from my assembly resolves the issue. However, when I re-include a JsonProvider in my project the error returns. I'm not sure that this is an issue with FSharp.Formatting, but any help is appreciated. Thanks

Here's the function from the scaffold generate.fsx where the error occurs.

```
// Build API reference from XML comments
let buildReference () =
  CleanDir (output @@ "reference")
  MetadataFormat.Generate
    ( binaries, output @@ "reference", layoutRootsAll.["en"],
      parameters = ("root", root)::info,
      sourceRepo = githubLink @@ "tree/master",
      sourceFolder = __SOURCE_DIRECTORY__ @@ ".." @@ "..",
      publicOnly = true,libDirs = libDirs )
```

title: FSharp.Formatting for F# 3.1 implicitly reading FSharp.Core 4.4.0.0
body:
I'm using the latest FSharp.Formatting on a Windows machine with both F# 4.0 and F# 3.1 installed. I'm running from a F# 3.1 command prompt.

I'm getting an error where the FSharp.Core 4.4.0.0 (from F# 4.0) is now being used by the FSharp.Formatting toolchain (for example the error below).  This is a bug - the FSharp.Formatting toolchain is currently built on FSharp.Compiler.Service for F# 3.1 and should never be reading FSharp.Core 4.4.0.0 unless explicitly directed to do so.

Has the change to Yaaf.FSharp.Scripting caused this?  Or is it an implicit reference to FSharp.Core 4.4.0.0 flowing from elsewhere, e.g. FCS itself?  Either way the FSharp.Formatting tooling feels somewhat broken right now, or at least not working in an isolated way,

I feel we need to move to a model where the reference set being used by interactive evaluation with FSharp.Formatting is more locked down.

```
    unknown(1,1): error FS0229: Error opening binary file 'C:\Program Files (x86)\Reference Assemblies\Microsoft\FSharp\.NETFramework\v4.0\4.4.0.0\FSharp.Co re.dll': Error reading/writing metadata for the F# compiled DLL 'C:\Program Files (x86)\Reference Assemblies\Microsoft\FSharp\.NETFramework\v4.0\4.4.0.0\FSharp.Core.dll'. ...).
```

cheers
don

title: Add `include-fsi` for F# Interactive output
body:
Dear colleagues.

In preparing the presentation "Overview FSharp 4.0" using [FsReveal](http://fsprojects.github.io/FsReveal/) in part "Rational exponents in units of measure" used the structure:

``` fsharp
open FSharp.Data.UnitSystems.SI.UnitSymbols 

[<Measure>] type cm 

(*** define-output:show-simple-measure ***)
let threeCm = 3<cm>

(*** include-output:show-simple-measure ***)
```

 Expecting to receive the output:

```
val threeCm : int<cm> = 3
```

But a blank line:

```

```

**PS:** Of course, in this situation, I came out of the situation and put the output in the form of constants. But, in my opinion, the document with a rich use of the measures would not be convenient.

title: Inherit IDisposable in IFsiEvaluator
body: Continuation of https://github.com/matthid/Yaaf.FSharp.Scripting/issues/5.

title: Markdown parser doesn't recognize CodeBlock inside QuotedBlock
body:
Look at this piece of markdown, and how it renders, and then how it parses in FSharp.Markdown:

``````
> __Annotation__
> This is an annnotation
> ```vb
> Module Module1
> End Module
> ```
> More annotation
``````

---

> **Annotation**
> This is an annnotation
> 
> ``` vb
> Module Module1
> End Module
> ```
> 
> More annotation

---

But FSharp.Markdown parses the quoted block like this:
-   QuotedBlock
  -   Paragraph
    -   Strong
      - Literal "Annotation"
    -   Literal "This is an annotation”
    -   InlineCode "`vb\nModule Module1\nEnd Module"
  -   Literal "More annotation"

It should parse it as a CodeBlock, but it's actually parsing it as an InlineCode.

title: Wrong indentation for paragraph within sublist
body:
Given this markdown

```
1.  List item

    1. Subone

    Paragraph

    7. SubRestart

5.  Another list item
```

then GitHub and commonmark [render it](http://spec.commonmark.org/dingus/?text=1.%20%20List%20item%0A%0A%20%20%20%201.%20Subone%0A%0A%20%20%20%20Paragraph%0A%0A%20%20%20%207.%20SubRestart%0A%0A5.%20%20Another%20list%20item) where the first item "List item" has _three_ children: (1) an ordered sublist "[subone]", (2) a paragraph, (3) an ordered sublist "[subrestart]". Here's a picture:
![commonmark](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3316258/10548752/abbececa-73f1-11e5-95b5-2b947788c35f.png)

But FSharp.Markdown instead parses it so the first item "List item" only has _one_ child: an ordered sublist whose first item is "subone / paragraph" and whose second item is "subrestart". Here's a picture:
![fsharp](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3316258/10548755/b16fc19e-73f1-11e5-9b59-c19ce576c67a.png)

title: Strikethrough markdown syntax not supported
body:
The following strikethrough syntax `~~FOO~~` with double tildes is not currently supported.
- The expected result is: ~~FOO~~
- The actual result is: ~~FOO~~

title: Markdown with image in header generates undesired HTML tags in page title
body: If the header line of a markdown file contains an image, like ``` # ![my logo](logo.jpg)  My title ``` then a `` tag gets added to the title of the generated HTML page: ``` <img src="logo.jpg" alt="my logo" /> My title ``` instead of the desirable outcome: ``` My title ```

title: processDirectory should support .cshtml files
body: Since .md and .fsx files are already going through the razor processing pipeline it would make sense to also support raw .cshtml files in processDirectory. This can be useful when using FSharp.Formatting to generate websites and blogs.

title: Allow definition of properties in markdown for use during Razor processing
body:
Right now it does not seem possible to define properties (like `@Foo`) within a markdown page and refer to it from a razor template. Such feature would for instance allow blog scenarios where metadata like blog post dates and author name are defined in the markdown itself and typeset in the razor template. 
Current workaround is to add a javascript snippet in the markdown defining global variables with values to be passed to the razor template and then render the content dynamically after the page is fully loaded using a windows.onLoad handler but this is ugly and introduces a delay in the rendering.

title: processDirectory should pass the relative path to the site root on each call to the Razor template
body:
When using processDirectory with a given razor template, the razor template may refer to various static resources (like css and images). Because files generated by processDirectory may reside in different subdirectories the template cannot rely on relative paths to refer to static resources. Instead one has to use absolute URLs generated using the @Root variable. The problem is that this prevents you from using the same set of HTML files for local view or when publishing to an online website.
It would be nice instead if processDirectory could generate a property containing the relative path from the output directory of the processed file to the root of the site.

title: Literate.ProcessMarkdown should write to Stream instead of disk file
body: HTML generators like `Literate.ProcessMarkdown` and `Literate.ProcessDocument` should ideally write the output to a .Net `Stream` rather than a file on disk. This would allow in-memory processing and efficient composition of different stream processors.

title: Improve exception handling in literate programming
body:
I've tried `include-value`, `include-output`, and `include-it`. None work if I throw in a `Json.parse` or `Json.tryParse` in the same evaluation block. I've even tried re-defining it in the local file and I still can't get any value other than `No value has been returned`. Any ideas where I am going wrong?

``` fsharp
(** Min repro: *)

title: Markdown: Underscores in the middle of a word translate as emphasis
body: I can't find a part of the CommonMark spec that explicitly states it rather than just being implied by the [left/right flanking delimiter](http://spec.commonmark.org/0.23/#left-flanking-delimiter-run) rules, but emphasis with the underscore like `some_words_here` should be translated as `some_words_here`, but come out of FSharp.Markdown as `somewordshere`. Spec has a [few examples of this](http://spec.commonmark.org/0.23/#example-326); makes snake-case value names output wrong :grimacing: Edit: Forgot links

title: It would be nice if reference documentation for types included implemented interfaces
body:
When `MetadataFormat.Generate` runs, the reference doesn't include implemented interfaces. 

This is especially problematic if you have interfaces defined by other interfaces, which is occasionally useful.  The resulting documentation, such as [this example](http://reedcopsey.github.io/Gjallarhorn/reference/gjallarhorn-idisposableview-1.html) , is far less useful than if it were to list out the interfaces that are implemented/inherited.

title: Raw Command does not work
body:
In the [Evaluation](http://tpetricek.github.io/FSharp.Formatting/evaluation.html) section there is a mention of a "raw" command. But this is not working. We should be able to write something like this.

```
(**
    (*** raw ***)
    Some raw text.
*)
```

And this would just emit the raw text

```
Some raw text.
```

Currently it will produce a block that is interpreted as an F# Snippet.

I Debugged the problem up to the function  `FSharp.Literate.CodeBlockUtils.collectComments`. In this function there is a Pattern matching for this kind of case.

```
| (ConcatenatedComments (String.StartsAndEndsWith ("(***", "***)") (ParseCommands cmds)))::lines ->
```

But this line will never match. Because the matched String is "    (**\* raw ***)" (with leading whitespace). Adding a `String.TrimStart` will break some other tests in the system. It seems that currently there doesn't exists any way to emit raw text. Neither from fsx or md.

title: Markdown parser doesn't recognize inline code `x | y` inside table cell
body:
The Markdown parser doesn't parse this table correctly:

| alpha | beta |
| --- | --- |
| `x + y` | plus |
| `x | y` | or |

I think it should be rendered as a table with three rows, the bottom two rows having inline-code as their first cells. This indeed is how GitHub renders it. It's also how the CommonMark demonstrator renders it at http://commonmark.unicorn.fail/ . (However, I couldn't find any mention of tables in the official CommonMark spec, and the official try.commonmark.com doesn't render tables at all).

But the FSharp markdown parser parses it very differently. It parses it as a table with just TWO rows, and the final row gets turned into a PARAGRAPH.

title: Markdown parser gets multiple-underscores-inside-italics wrong
body:
The markdown parser gets underscores-inside-italics wrong. For instance,

```
A *asterisk_one_two* B
```

On both GitHub and CommonMark, it's rendered as an italic with underscores:

A _asterisk_one_two_ B

But FSharp.Formatting.Markdown parses it as a nested emphasis:

```
Emphasis [ Literal "asterisk", Emphasis [ Literal "one"], Literal "two"]
```

(Actually, to be honest, I don't know what is the correct behavior. I'm assuming that CommonMark is correct here.)

Here are some more examples:

```
A *asterisk_one_two* B

A *asterisk_one_two_three* B

A *asterisk_one_two_three_four* B

A _underscore_one_two_ B

A _underscore_one_two_three_ B

A _underscore_one_two_three_four_ B
```

A _asterisk_one_two_ B

A _asterisk_one_two_three_ B

A _asterisk_one_two_three_four_ B

A _underscore_one_two_ B

A _underscore_one_two_three_ B

A _underscore_one_two_three_four_ B

title: Markdown parser gets asterisk inside codeblock inside list wrong

labels.name: help wanted
labels.description: Tasks that are up for grabs!

body:
The markdown parser gets asterisk-inside-codeblock-inside-list wrong. For instance, try this markdown code

``````
*   Alpha

    ```
    *   Beta
    ```

    Gamma
``````

On both GitHub and CommonMark, it's rendered as a bulleted list with only one item; that item contains text, then a codeblock, then more text:

---
-   Alpha
  
  ```
  *   Beta
  ```
  
  Gamma

---

But F# mistakes the asterisk for a sub-bulleted-item, and so it produces

```
   ListBlock[
      Paragraph "Alpha"
      CodeBlock ""
      ListBlock[
         Paragraph "Beta"
         CodeBlock "Gamma"
```

which renders like this:

---
-   Alpha
  
  ```
  <empty>
  ```
  -   Beta
    
    ```
    Gamma
    ```

---

title: Markdown extensions
body:
I've just started using Fsharp.formatting to process markdown files defining a set of tutorial worksheets for a new F# lecture course at my university next year. It is great having the compiler-generated tooltips and coloring. I have enough time to get the infrastructure right, so I'm wondering how best to do the other things I need.

This issue is because I don't want to reimplement the wheel and would be happy to add to the core code if that was merited (though I doubt it will be). I guess this is a meta-discussion about how to compose non-standard features.

Previously (another language) I was using Pandoc markdown and transforming this to PDF and HTML with Pandoc, where the HTML output was important, and PDF an added bonus but maybe not perfectly formatted.

The features I need and (I think) have not got are:
- footnotes (as in pandoc and other extensions)
- hyperlinks to additional pages with material for page extracted from the original MD file. I don't know a standard way to do this but for me the utility outweighs the trouble of implementing it myself. The use case is answers to questions in the main file (Pandoc did not do this but I often wanted it and will add the feature now).

So my questions are for these two features:
1. do they exist at the moment?
2. does anyone have any advice as to which way to implement if this is done outside the formatting core code? there is a lot of scope for pre-processing input, or processing the formatting parse tree.
3. should additional features like these be published for others with the formatting code, or is it not worth it?

Best wishes, Tom

title: Single quote in function name, turns code in to red.
body:
This is a character: `'a'`

But this is a function:

```
let myloop() =
   let rec myloop' acc
      myloop' (acc+1)
   myloop' 0
```

Something goes wrong here:

title: Ugly flicker when tooltip overlaps parent element
body:
When FSharp.Formatting is used to generate HTML markup, its tooltips look fine if they don't overlap their parent element. But if, due to their positioning on the page or the CSS used to style them, they end up overlapping their parent element, some ugly flicker is visible as the mouse pointer moves over the parent element. Here's a visual example from [FsReveal](https://github.com/fsprojects/FsReveal), which uses FSharp.Formatting to produce its slides:

![bad](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/90762/15273888/5ec751f6-1ad0-11e6-88db-a7943b7d891d.gif)

That's from FsReveal's sample presentation, with no changes made. The mouse cursor failed to show up in this animated screen capture, but I was moving it back and forth between the name `factorial` and the `let` just to the left.

This problem is also visible in the FSharp.Formatting documentation, although it's harder to see it happening there because the tooltips _almost_ don't overlap their parent elements. But go to http://tpetricek.github.io/FSharp.Formatting/literate.html and hover the mouse over any F# name that has a tooltip (say, `printfn` in the first code sample). The tooltip (at least on my browser) overlaps the parent `span` by one row of pixels, making it possible to trigger the flickering by moving the mouse very precisely left and right alone that one row of pixels. Like so:

![literate](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/90762/15273957/fe3fc974-1ad1-11e6-95e0-27ffbf2dc7e6.gif)

The cause of this issue is that when the tooltip element appears, it naturally lies above its parent element in z-index order and thus the browser considers it to be what the mouse cursor is currently pointing to. When the element appears, as long as the cursor hasn't moved yet, no `mouseover` or `mouseout` events will fire. But the moment the cursor moves even one pixel, the browser sees it as over a new element: it _was_ over the parent `span`, and now it's over the tooltip `div`. So the browser fires a `mouseout` event on the parent `span` (which causes the tooltip to be hidden) and a `mouseover` event on the tooltip `div` (which does nothing). And now the tooltip is hidden, so the next time the cursor moves, a `mouseout` will fire on the tooltip `div` (doing nothing) and a `mouseover` will fire on the parent `span` (showing the tooltip again). And that cycle repeats all over again, causing the tooltip `div` to alternate between shown and hidden with every update of the mouse pointer. Result: highly-annoying flickering.

There are two possible solutions to this issue. One is to take advantage of the little-known `pointer-events` CSS property. An element with `pointer-events: none` will be ignored for the purpose of `mouseover` and `mouseout` events (and other mouse events as well). This is the simplest solution since it only involves adding a single line of CSS to the `.tip` class, and it works on modern versions of all browsers. It does **not**, however, work on IE versions 10 or earlier; only IE 11+ will honor `pointer-events: none` properly on HTML elements, according to the http://caniuse.com/#feat=pointer-events chart.

That solution produces the following result:

![good](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/90762/15273890/6d15521c-1ad0-11e6-853c-d894a3e3e99f.gif)

It's not quite obvious in that animated GIF, but the tooltip disappears as soon as the mouse moves off the word `factorial`. It would be more obvious if I had moved the mouse more slowly, but the tooltip can disappear while the mouse cursor is still over the tooltip, as long as the cursor has moved off the parent element (in that example, the `span` containing the word `factorial`).

Another possible solution would be to change the logic of the tooltips in Javascript: instead of disappearing when the mouse leaves the parent element, make the tooltip stay visible as long as the mouse is inside the parent element _or_ inside the tooltip. In other words, the tooltip would behave like this:

![also-good](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/90762/15274299/c4ea2578-1ad8-11e6-88fd-70e6f04e5f01.gif)

Again, it may not be obvious, but here the tooltip stays visible even when the mouse cursor has left the parent `span`.

The drawback of this approach is that if the code in question has a lot of invocations of common functions like `List.reduce` or `Seq.ofList`, the tooltips for `List` and `Seq` will tend to be quite large -- and it's possible that someone trying to hover the mouse pointer over `reduce` will accidentally brush it over the name `List` first, and then have to move the pointer quite far away before they get off the tooltip. At which point getting back to the word `reduce` can become an exercise in frustration, as any module name becomes a landmine the mouse pointer has to avoid.

Therefore, I would suggest the CSS fix (adding `pointer-events: none` to the tooltip CSS). Its only drawback is that it won't work in IE versions before 11. But if anyone is running IE 10 or earlier, they really should upgrade anyway, so I don't find that to be a major issue. Therefore, my recommendation would be to use the CSS fix rather than the Javascript fix.

title: Can't generate documentation on Linux: "Failed to import assembly 'Argu, Version=2.1.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=null'
body:
There seems to be a problem running FSharp.Formatting's `generate.fsx` script on Linux. I have consistently been able to reproduce the same results: the first run will succeed. (Well, sort of -- there's another bug that's being triggered, but see below for details). Then the second run will fail with an error about "Failed to import assembly Argu", and that's the bug that I'm trying to report right now.

The first clue that the "Failed to import assembly Argu" error is going to show up is if, when running `./build.sh GenerateDocs`, the compilation output includes the line:

> Redirect assembly from 'FSharp.Compiler.Service, Version=2.0.0.3, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=null' to 'FSharp.Compiler.Service, Version=2.0.0.6, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=null'

If this assembly redirect appears, it will soon be followed by:

> Compilation errors:
> - error: (0, 0) Unhandled Exception:
> - error: (0, 0) Mono.CSharp.InternalErrorException: Failed to import assembly `Argu, Version=2.1.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=null' ---> System.ArgumentException: Value does not fall within the expected range.
> - error: (0, 0)   at IKVM.Reflection.Fusion.CompareAssemblyIdentityPure (System.String assemblyIdentity1, Boolean unified1, System.String assemblyIdentity2, Boolean unified2, IKVM.Reflection.AssemblyComparisonResult& result) <0x7f23f8ead540 + 0x00c43> in <filename unknown>:0 
> - error: (0, 0)   at IKVM.Reflection.Universe.CompareAssemblyIdentity (System.String assemblyIdentity1, Boolean unified1, System.String assemblyIdentity2, Boolean unified2, IKVM.Reflection.AssemblyComparisonResult& result) <0x7f23f8ed66a0 + 0x0006e> in <filename unknown>:0 
> - error: (0, 0)   at Mono.CSharp.StaticLoader.AssemblyReferenceResolver (System.Object sender, IKVM.Reflection.ResolveEventArgs args) <0x7f23f8e1ab80 + 0x0010d> in <filename unknown>:0 
> - error: (0, 0)   at IKVM.Reflection.Universe.Load (System.String refname, IKVM.Reflection.Module requestingModule, Boolean throwOnError) <0x7f23f8ed5ba0 + 0x0010a> in <filename unknown>:0 

(snip the rest of the stacktrace, as it contains no line numbers and will probably not be useful. I can post the whole thing if it would help.)
### Steps to reproduce
1. Get an Ubuntu Trusty (14.04) or Linux Mint 18 (based on Ubuntu Xenial 16.04) machine, and install the latest version of Mono (4.4.1) by following the instructions at http://www.mono-project.com/docs/getting-started/install/linux/
2. `git clone https://github.com/tpetricek/FSharp.Formatting`
3. `cd FSharp.Formatting`
4. `./build.sh` - NOTE: On some systems, this seems to trigger fsharp/FAKE#1196. I believe that bug is unrelated to the one I'm reporting now -- but the source of that encoding mismatch also needs to be tracked down.
5. `./build.sh GenerateDocs` - will **fail** with a `Mono.CSharp.InternalErrorException`.
6. `rm -rf docs/tools/.fake && ./build.sh GenerateDocs` will "succeed". (Sort of -- right now it triggers fsharp/FAKE#1196, but it bypasses the "Failed to import assembly Argu" error that this bug report is about).

Clearly, the cached build script that FAKE produces has an assembly reference to FSharp.Compiler.Service 2.0.0.3, but its current version (which Paket downloads) is 2.0.0.6. Somehow, this is triggering a bug in Mono's assembly resolver that makes it unable to load Argu, and things blow up.

I believe the FSharp.Compiler.Service 2.0.0.3 reference is coming from FSharpVSPowerTools, because I see the following in `paket.lock` after building FSharp.Formatting:

```
FSharpVSPowerTools.Core (2.3)
  FSharp.Compiler.Service (>= 2.0.0.3)
```

However, I've been unable to trace this bug any further. I don't know why the assembly redirect is triggering a failure on importing Argu. All I know is that FSharp.Formatting's `generate.fsx` script seems to be at the heart of this bug.
### Miscellaneous notes

I've actually been seeing this bug for some time, before I ever saw fsharp/FAKE#1196 being triggered. The way I used to see it was to check out a clean copy of https://github.com/fsprojects/ProjectScaffold, run its `./build.sh` once to give the project a name, then try to run `./build.sh GenerateHelp` twice. The first run would succeed, and the second one would fail with this bug. However, if I do that now, the first run triggers fsharp/FAKE#1196 and then the second one triggers this bug.

title: FSharp.Formatting vNext
body:
The current implementation of FSharp.Formatting is highly dependent upon Razor and isn't setup in an easily extensible manner. I think this has held back the usefulness of FSharp.Formatting, and limited the contexts and types of documents its been used to create.     

[I think we could use the architecture of DocFx as inspiration for a more powerful FSharp.Formatting](http://dotnet.github.io/docfx/tutorial/intro_template.html)

![](http://dotnet.github.io/docfx/spec/images/docfx_workflow_highlevel.png)

Reorienting FSharp.Formatting as a documentation production pipeline that can support multiple input parsers for a variety of formats (e.g. specific extended markdown formats) that can feed into multiple output producers could extend FSharp.Formatting's range into epubs, pdfs, or any other format users are willing to build support for.

One of the main things I'd like to work towards is improving liquid templating support and providing a wider range of options for producing API pages. If these could be packaged with Fable plugins to add features like search and search via type signature we could greatly improve the usability of F# API pages. Taking it a step further we could set up documentation production so that it fully includes source code tied to a specific release version and have sourcelink link to these static source code release pages so that the links would always take you to the source code that produced the dll. This could be improved even further by adding full intellisense and systax highlighting to the static source pages that are produced. The most extreme version of these advanced API pages could be created with a limited version of monaco built in to enable find all references, code peek, and many of the other editor features we've come to love in vscode.
## Template Processing
- [Handlebars Markup Template](http://handlebarsjs.com/)
  - Processor - [Handlebars.Net](https://github.com/rexm/Handlebars.Net)  
## Reference Libraries
- [SourceBrowser](https://github.com/KirillOsenkov/SourceBrowser)
- [DocFx](https://github.com/dotnet/docfx)
- [SourceLink](https://github.com/ctaggart/SourceLink)
## Into The Future

[A dotnet cli tool for documentation production](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/articles/core/tools/extensibility)

title: Feature request: index of all functions in assembly
body:
One of the things that I have often wished to have while reading through the API reference of various F# libraries is that there was an index of all functions in a given assembly, no matter what namespace or module those functions were found in. This is especially needed for [FAKE](http://fsharp.github.io/FAKE/apidocs/index.html), whose API docs [badly need an index](https://github.com/fsharp/FAKE/issues/1485). The best tool to create such an index is, of course, FSharp.Formatting, the same tool that created those API docs in the first place.

What I'd like to see in a function index is:

1. All public functions in the namespace are listed in the index, in alphabetical order.
1. Function names are sorted case-insensitively. (In the invariant culture, so that running a build that includes FSharp.Formatting will always produce the same index file no matter what culture the build server is located in.)
1. All function names starting with the same *base* letter (i.e., after NFKD decomposition and stripping any diacritics), case-insensitively, will be grouped into one section. I.e., the functions `allWidgets`, `AdvancedQuark` and `ÅngströmToMeter` would all be grouped into section `A`. Since most code bases use the Latin alphabet for function names even when their native language uses a different alphabet, that means in most cases, there will be 26 sections, `A` through `Z`. (Some, like `Q` and `X`, are likely to be empty). But the implementation should be prepared to cope with any number of sections, and not just assume that there will be a maximum of 26 sections.
1. All function names that do not start with a letter (e.g., operators) are grouped in their own section, separately from the names that do start with letters. So for a code base written with the Latin alphabet, that means there would likely be 27 sections at most. The non-letter section would be named something like `!@#`, though perhaps `(operators)` would also be a good section name.
1. Each index section would produce its own HTML page, or there could also be an all-in-one HTML page that listed *all* functions. There could be advantages or disadvantages either way, and I'm not sure yet which would be best.
1. At the top and bottom of each page, and in between each section in the all-in-one index page, would be a list of links that would take you to each section. E.g., `A B C D E ... X Y Z   (operators)`, where each letter is either a page-internal link (in the case of the all-in-one index page) or a link to the appropriate section page.
1. Each function in the index has an indicator of which module it comes from, and whether its module has the `AutoOpen` attribute. This is particularly important in the case of the FAKE documentation, where it's currently very hard to tell which functions are immediately available by opening the `Fake` namespace. (At least, it's hard to tell that in the absence of good Intellisense from Ionide or similar IDEs. But FAKE scripts are the piece of F# code most likely to be edited without the benefit of an IDE, and FAKE is the project most desperately in need of an index).

As you can tell, this feature request is primarily driven by the needs of the FAKE documentation, but I think this will be a valuable feature for other projects' API docs as well.

title: NuGet package does not match TargetVersion in assemblies
body:
The assemblies inside [FSharp.Formatting/3.0.0-beta01](https://www.nuget.org/packages/FSharp.Formatting/3.0.0-beta01) live under: lib\net40 but the assemblies inside are actually compiled against .NET 4.5.  The assemblies in master branch are compiled against .NET 4.6.1.

**Repro Steps:**
1. Download [FSharp.Formatting/3.0.0-beta01](https://www.nuget.org/packages/FSharp.Formatting/3.0.0-beta01) 
2. Unzip package
3. `ildasm .\fsharp.formatting.3.0.0-beta01\lib\net40\FSharp.Formatting.Common.dll`
4. Examine the manifest and look for `TargetFrameworkAttribute`

I can help fix this issue. I encountered this issue when trying to fix https://github.com/Antaris/RazorEngine/pull/480#issuecomment-316985979.  
The reason the that RazorEngine build step is broken is because FSharp.Formatting references System.Web.Razor but the PR uses Microsoft.AspNetCore.Razor.  I want to migrate this project to use RazorEngine.Razor. What do you think @matthid ?

title: Source Linking does not link to other repositories like TFS correctly
body:
When providing a sourceRepo to the MetadataFormat: 
```
MetadataFormat.Generate
    ( binaries, output @@ "reference", layoutRootsAll.["en"],
      parameters = ("root", root)::info,
      sourceRepo = tfsLink, //setting up source link to go to tfs instead of git hub
      sourceFolder = __SOURCE_DIRECTORY__ @@ ".." @@ "..",
      publicOnly = true,libDirs = libDirs )
```

When building the file links it does not properly handle URL parameters. 
For instance a correct URL would be:
 _`https://**link**/_git/repo/?path=**filepath**&line=**LineNumber**`, however even when specifying the `/?path=`_ 
The formatted Url when providing the tfsLink as _`https://**link**/_git/repo/?path`_ is: 
_`https://**link**/_git/repo//**filepath**?path=#L66-66`_
Where the bolded terms are things I would like control over. The **filepath** is something that is known from from the DLL's as is the **LineNumber**, but the rest of the formatting is predefined and only works for Git, Path.Combine does not properly handle url parameters.

title: FSharp.Formatting error while using FSharp.Data's CsvProvider in ProjectScaffold.

### Description

Adding `type Stocks = CsvProvider<"./data/MSFT.csv">` to `https://github.com/fsprojects/ProjectScaffold/blob/master/src/FSharp.ProjectTemplate/Library.fs` causes a FSharp.Formatting Error

### Repro steps

Please provide the steps required to reproduce the problem

1.a Use `forge new scaffold` to create a new Scaffold.
1.b Add `nuget FSharp.Data` to `group Build` in `paket.dependencies`: https://github.com/fsprojects/ProjectScaffold/blob/master/paket.dependencies#L18

```fsharp
namespace ProcessingData

open FSharp.Data

/// Stock prices example
type Stocks = CsvProvider<"./data/MSFT.csv">

/// Documentation for my library
///
/// ## Example
///
///     let h = Library.hello 1
///     printfn "%d" h
///
module Library = 
  /// Returns 42
  ///
  /// ## Parameters
  ///  - `num` - whatever

```

open System.IO

title: Literate file generation: template changes not detected when filtering documents
body:
Hello,

*[A minor edge case I bumped into, so mostly just an FYI for other people upgrading to the awesome v3... :)]*

Template changes are not detected when filtering documents for Literate documentation generation.

Literate.ProcessDirectory filters documents based on a comparison of the write dates of the input/output files before generating new documentation.  Changes to *templates* are therefore 'ignored' until the input documents are edited or the output docs deleted.  To an unknowing user it appears like the library is doing nothing (returns unit).

v3 is looking great! :D

---

Reproduction: change a template, run Literate.ProcessDirectory, see documentation is unchanged 

Expected:  updated documents

Workaround: edit the input documents, delete the output documents, or reset their LastWriteTime

[Cause:](https://github.com/fsprojects/FSharp.Formatting/blame/c35585da271a32c72d423dccc613658f61bbc096/src/FSharp.Literate/Main.fs#L210)

```
            // Update only when needed
            let changeTime = File.GetLastWriteTime(file)
            let generateTime = File.GetLastWriteTime(output)
            if changeTime > generateTime then
```

title: Can’t avoid line numbers with command line tool
body:
The command line tool has `—lineNumbers` which defaults to `true`. Unfortunately that means you effectively can not turn it off looks like  - because CommandLineParser interprets absence of the arg as the default, and presence of the arg as true also. 

Lmk if I missed anything here. 

If not, any preference for changing the default to false, OR changing the switch to `—noLineNumbers`? Happy to PR either.
